description: In put(), line 297 gets bytes: Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> from value.into_bytes(). Line 310 does Zeroizing::new(bytes.to_vec()) creating a redundant second copy. Line 311 does owned.as_slice().to_vec() creating a third allocation passed to MaxNvBuffer::try_from(). Since MaxNvBuffer internally wraps Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> (confirmed in tss-esapi 7.7.0 buffer_type\! macro), there is NO zeroization leak — all copies are protected. However, the 'owned' intermediate is completely redundant: you could go directly from bytes.to_vec() to MaxNvBuffer::try_from(). The comment 'Wrap in Zeroizing so the copy is zeroed on drop' is misleading because MaxNvBuffer already handles zeroization internally. Fix: replace lines 309-311 with 'let data = MaxNvBuffer::try_from(bytes.to_vec())' and update the comment. This is a clarity/waste issue, not a security bug. Downgraded to P2.
title: put() has redundant unzeroized copy chain
